name: Bella Feldman
bio: Bella Tabak Feldman was an American sculptor who worked in forged steel and blown glass for more than six decades. Her subjects were war, sexuality, and the tensions of industrial life.
quote: I produce anxious objects, yet humor laces through my work because, like my ghetto forebears, I need to laugh at the dark.

sections.heading: Early Life & Education
sections.text:
Born Bella R. Tabak in 1930 in New York City, Feldman grew up in Bronx tenements, the daughter of working-class Polish-Jewish immigrants.¹ She attended the High School of Music & Art in Manhattan during World War II. Admission was by portfolio review; it was one of the few public schools in the country dedicated to training young artists.¹

At thirteen she visited the Museum of Modern Art, where she encountered Meret Oppenheim's Object (1936) — a fur-covered teacup, saucer, and spoon. She later credited the experience with awakening her to the psychological power of sculpture.¹ She was also drawn to Alberto Giacometti's The Palace at 4 am (1932) and the work of David Smith. Her earliest sculpture, Warrior (1952), was a direct homage to Giacometti.¹

The Holocaust took members of her family who had remained in Poland. The loss shaped her lifelong preoccupation with war and the military-industrial complex.¹ ⁴

Feldman earned a BA from Queens College, City University of New York. She married Leonard Feldman at eighteen, and the couple moved to California in 1951.¹ She completed an MA in sculpture at San Jose State University in 1973, studying under Sam Richardson, John Battenberg, and Fletcher Benton.¹ ²

sections.heading: Career & Teaching
sections.text:
In 1965, Feldman joined the faculty at the California College of the Arts (then California College of Arts and Crafts). She taught there for thirty-six years, until 2001, chairing the Sculpture Department in 1987–89 and again in 1996, serving as Graduate Director in 1987, and ultimately being named Professor Emeritus.¹ ²

From 1972 to 1975 she taught art at Makerere University in Kampala, Uganda, initially on an E.L. Cabot Trust Fund grant from Harvard University. She arrived the year after Idi Amin's coup and left as conditions in the country continued to deteriorate. The experience marked her deeply: it sharpened both her political consciousness and her engagement with African art and material culture. In 1983, she and Dr. Ross Coates co-authored "The So Cattle Sculpture of Uganda" for African Arts, published by UCLA Press — a scholarly reflection on the sculptural traditions she had encountered at Makerere.¹ ²

On returning to CCA, she faced gender discrimination and a threat to her position. She retained her job and went on to advocate for other women faculty members, helping them secure equity and job security.¹ ⁶

Her feminist commitments extended beyond the institution. In 1974, she showed in the Taboo Show at Womanspace in Los Angeles, one of the earliest feminist art spaces in the country — an exhibition dedicated to work that challenged social and artistic prohibitions.²

She founded the Sculpture Program at Santa Rosa Junior College, where she taught from 1975 to 1979. Her intellectual range extended beyond the studio: in 1984 she published "A View of Evolution Expressed in Sculpture: A Reflection of Nuclear Angst" in Leonardo, the journal of the International Society of Arts, Sciences and Technology — an essay connecting her sculptural forms to evolutionary biology and nuclear anxiety. She contributed again to Leonardo's 1992 theme publication on Women, Art and Technology.² ⁸

In 1989, she toured three art colleges in Japan as a visiting lecturer — part of a pattern of international engagement that had begun in Uganda and would continue throughout her career. The following year, in 1990, she returned to Makerere University as a Cultural Specialist for the United States Information Service, fifteen years after her first departure. Uganda was no longer under Amin, and Feldman returned to the institution where she had first taught on the African continent.²

Her visiting positions spanned four decades and ranged from the University of Iowa (1979, 1984) and the University of Hawaii at Manoa to the Chicago Art Institute, Massachusetts College of Art, and Arizona State University. She was twice a guest artist at Pilchuck Glass School in Washington State (2003 and 2014), working at the intersection of glass and metal that defined her late career.²

She had two children: Nina (born 1954) and Ethan (born 1956).¹

sections.heading: Artistic Philosophy & Materials
sections.text:
Feldman worked with structure, decay, and renewal through discarded and industrial materials. Her subjects — sexuality, war, industrial anxiety — placed her in conversation with Surrealism, Post-Minimalism, and Feminist art, though she claimed no formal affiliation with any movement.¹

"I combined glass and metal to suggest vulnerability and constraint as well as seduction."¹

She worked in forged steel, blown glass, found objects, braided cable, wire mesh, bronze, and diamond plate steel. Her signature technique, developed in the late 1990s, involved blowing glass directly into forged metal forms.¹ ³

Critics have noted parallels with Eva Hesse in her exploration of material tension — the binding, bulging, and constraining of forms.¹

sections.heading: Major Series
sections.text:
Metamorphosis (1970s) — Small-scale cast metal sculptures of animal transformations: rats becoming fish, turtles with human features, shown in large clusters. Her Birds (1970), cast metal dead birds, preceded Kiki Smith's Jersey Crows (1995) by twenty-five years. Metamorphic Turtles (1973–75) predated Smith's Sirens and Harpies (2002).¹

War Toys (1992) — A response to the first Gulf War. Feldman objected to President George H.W. Bush's tone when discussing Patriot missiles. The series has been compared to Magdalena Abakanowicz's War Games (1989) and shares concerns with Nancy Spero's The War Series (1966–70) and Judith Bernstein's Iraq Travel Poster (1969).¹

Flasks of Fiction (1998–2001) — Her first series combining blown glass with forged metal armatures. The hanging sculptures were inspired by mosque lanterns she saw while traveling in Turkey.¹ ³

War Toys Redux (2003) — A return to the War Toys theme, now incorporating blown glass with steel. Feldman described the glass forms as "effeminizing" the aggressive objects.¹

Large-Scale Sculpture (2003 onward) — Works combining metal, glass, and machine parts at architectural scale. Includes Dyad (2003) and Jacob's Ladder (2011).¹

sections.heading: Architectural Commissions
sections.text:
Alongside her studio practice, Feldman maintained a parallel career in architectural commissions over the course of several decades. The work ranged across media — mosaic, relief, sculptural fountain — and reveals a side of her practice rarely discussed in gallery contexts.²

Her largest public commissions were a sculpture fountain for the Federal Home Loan Bank lobby at 600 California Street in San Francisco and a series of garden wall panels for IBM in San Jose, designed in collaboration with architect John Bolles. For the Barclay Simpson Garden in Orinda, she created a sculpture fountain within a landscape by Ron Lutsko. These corporate and institutional works demanded a scale and permanence that her gallery sculpture did not.²

She also worked in mosaic — a medium she did not use in her studio art. She designed a tile facade for Crystal Elementary School in Suisun City, a mosaic mural for the executive offices of the Lovable Brassiere Company in Atlanta, and a mosaic panel for a medical building in Redding. Her architectural reliefs appear at Temple Rodef Sholom in San Rafael (architects Marquis & Stoller), Mountain View City Hall, and the Science and Industrial Arts Building at Martin Luther King Jr. Middle School in Berkeley.²

Public sculpture commissions included works for Marina Vista Plaza in Vallejo and the Potrero Project in Richmond, both designed with the landscape firm Royston, Hanamoto & Mayes, and a sculpture for Hydronautics, Inc. outside Washington, D.C. In all, her CV lists at least twelve architectural projects — a body of work that placed her sculpture in the daily lives of people who would never set foot in a gallery.²

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.text:
Feldman's work was reviewed consistently for more than three decades, from the early 1980s through 2014 — an unusual span of sustained critical attention for a Bay Area sculptor.²

Kenneth Baker of the San Francisco Chronicle was her most persistent critic, reviewing her work at least six times between 1987 and 2013. He wrote about her scavenged-material sculpture in 1988 ("Home Grown Sculpture"), returned to it in 1992 ("Scavenged Material Becomes Sculpture"), and covered her retrospective in 2013.¹⁰ Peter Selz, the UC Berkeley art historian and former MoMA curator, wrote the essay "Solidity is Always Undermined" for Sculpture Magazine in 2014 — one of three features the magazine devoted to her work over sixteen years, alongside Maria Porges (1998) and Kathleen Whitney (2000).⁹ Leah Ollman of the Los Angeles Times praised the "metaphoric richness" of her steel and glass work in 1999.¹¹ Terri Cohn reviewed the fifty-year retrospective for Squarecylinder.com in 2013.¹² Her War Toys series drew national press: Sheila Farr in the Seattle Times (2004), Joy Hakanson Colby in the Detroit News (1994), and Marsha Miro in the Detroit Free Press (1994) all covered the traveling installations.²

In 2006, she was the cover story in Artist Blacksmith, a UK publication, bringing her forged steel practice to an international craft audience. In 2008, filmmaker Elizabeth Sher completed Bella, Bella, a documentary portrait of Feldman and her work.²

The Fresno Art Museum named her Distinguished Woman Artist of 1996, mounting a full survey of her sculpture from 1974 to 1996. The Richmond Art Center mounted a fifty-year retrospective in 2013, surveying work from 1991 to 2012.² ⁵

Bella Feldman died on May 6, 2024, at ninety-four, in Berkeley, California. She had lived and worked in Oakland, Berkeley, and London. Through her thirty-six-year tenure at CCA, her founding of the Santa Rosa Junior College sculpture program, and visiting positions from Pilchuck to the University of Hawaii, she taught multiple generations of sculptors. Her advocacy for women faculty at CCA in the 1970s, and her participation in early feminist art spaces like Womanspace in Los Angeles, extended her influence beyond the studio.¹ ⁶

Her work is represented by SculptureSite Gallery (now A New Leaf Gallery/Sculpturesite) in Sonoma, California.³
